trends.title: Jonathan Anderson Rewrites Dior
trends.summary: Jonathan Anderson's debut womenswear collection for Dior has become the most talked-about runway moment of the season, generating heated editorial debate across the industry. WWD described a collection built around what Anderson called a tension between 'harmony and tension' — opening with a white plissé lampshade dress that floated on invisible hoops, then moving through bubble dresses in vaporous lace, plissé satin tops with stiff lace collars, and wispy negligees in cage-like box constructions. Grazia noted that Anderson's signatures — whimsy, wit, and a willingness to challenge convention — were unmistakable, with denim minis paired with tuxedos, bubble skirts, bunny-eared pumps, and cabbage rose shoes signaling that, as editors put it, he is 'here to experiment, not embalm.'

trends.title: '80s Power Dressing Goes Glamoratti
trends.summary: Editors and trend forecasters across the board are declaring the end of quiet luxury and the arrival of '80s-inflected maximalism, now branded with the term 'Glamoratti.' Pinterest reported a 225% surge in searches for '80s luxury,' and designers from Saint Laurent to Versace and Thom Browne sent sculpted-shoulder blazers, color-blocked power suits, and oversized silhouettes down SS26 runways. Marie Claire noted that the core of this aesthetic is a baggy, sculpted-shoulder suit paired with chunky gold accessories and dramatic funnel necks — power dressing reframed for Gen Z ambition rather than boardroom conformity.

trends.title: Snakeskin as the New Neutral
trends.summary: After seasons of leopard domination, editors are declaring snakeskin the animal print of the moment — and treating it as a versatile neutral rather than a statement accent. Coveteur traced the momentum from Marni and Ludovic de Saint Sernin's FW25-26 shows through to SS26, where LaQuan Smith showed lightweight snakeskin blouses in bold colors and Khaite sent a parade of python bags down the runway. Who What Wear illustrated the trend's wearability via Amanda Seyfried at the Tory Burch show, pairing snakeskin Pierced heels with a gold top and icy-blue skirt — a mix that proved the print can function like a neutral against any palette.

trends.title: Sculptural Volume Dominates Summer Silhouettes
trends.summary: Across cruise, resort, and SS26 ready-to-wear collections, fashion editors have identified a decisive shift away from slinky minimalism toward dramatic, architectural volume. Runway Magazine noted that puffed skirts, structured blazers, and exaggerated proportions are the season's defining shapes, with designers pairing artful volume against clean, minimal styling to let the silhouette speak. Who What Wear confirmed the balloon trouser as a key crossover shape — shown at Balmain, Michael Kors, and Calvin Klein — while Simone Rocha, Erdem, and Stella McCartney have turned the hip into the new focal point via crinolines, draping, and shapely layering.

trends.title: Tomato Red and Cobalt Blue Lead the Color Conversation
trends.summary: Fashion critics are marking a clean break from the soft, neutral palettes of the quiet luxury era, with bold saturated hues taking over editorial coverage for 2026. Coveteur identified tomato red as the breakout color of both SS26 and FW26, appearing at Valentino, Carolina Herrera, and Balenciaga; the site also flagged deep eggplant — seen at Diotima and Cult Gaia — as an evening-wear alternative to black. Who What Wear pointed to cobalt and capri blue as the era-defining cool tone, taking over from the burgundy that peaked in 2025, and making the case for oceanic cerulean as the season's power statement.

trends.title: Bohemian Gets a Bolder, More Dynamic Update
trends.summary: The boho aesthetic that Chemena Kamali reignited at Chloé in her AW24 debut — centered on lace, creamy palettes, and floaty silhouettes — is now evolving into something more vibrant and dynamic. Who What Wear observed that Chloé and Zimmermann are driving a shift toward bolder patterns, watercolor florals, and more energetic silhouettes that still read as romantic but feel less languorous. Marie Claire also noted the wider lace and boho revival playing out across brands like Brandon Maxwell, Courrèges, Gucci, and Ulla Johnson — from lace-trimmed slip skirts to crochet appliqués — with a cultural energy rooted in the broader return of '70s-inflected softness and feminine sensuality.
